steps.title: Spot the author's deliberate caveat
steps.content: The strongest clue is the parenthetical: “although in Hans Castorp's favor it should be noted that it is his story, and that not every story happens to everybody.” Despite emphasizing the story is told “not for his sake,” the author credits Hans Castorp for being the subject of this remarkable story.

steps.title: Match the caveat to the answer choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Though it is true that stories of even the most uninteresting people are themselves interesting because all people are unique, the reason this story is interesting is nonetheless difficult to understand because of the passage of time.: Overstates the passage and misses the narrator's actual caveat about Hans Castorp's relation to the story. ✗
  • Choice B — Even though it is a story of a person of no particular importance, its age and the manner in which it therefore must be told are both indicators that the story itself is important.: Identifies the person as unimportant and sees the story's importance in its age and manner of telling, but overlooks the explicit credit given to the person. ✗
  • Choice C — It is a remarkable story that happened to an unremarkable person, though one could plausibly argue that because the story is valuable, some of its value accrues to the person at its center.: Captures the crucial logic: the story is valuable in itself, but some value also accrues to Hans Castorp because it is his story. ✓
  • Choice D — Like all stories about the lives of inconsequential people, this story must necessarily be related in a particular way if the reason the story is consequential is to be made evident to the audience.: Unsupported generalization; the passage does not claim that all such stories must be told in a particular way. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
